#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle Regime Repo - Validator Module

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBRR_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbrr multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBRR_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbrr_*)

zrbrr_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBRR_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbrr already kindled"

  # No defaults set — buv uses ${!varname:-} for safe indirect expansion under set -u.
  # Unset variables are detected distinctly from empty by zbuv_check_capture.

  # Enroll all RBRR variables — single source of truth for validation and rendering

  buv_regime_enroll RBRR

  buv_group_enroll "Resource Prefixing"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX         2   11  "Prefix for cloud-visible resource names (depot project, GAR repo, GCS bucket, pool stem)"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX       2   11  "Prefix prepended to local container and network names"

  buv_group_enroll "Vessel and Local Configuration"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRR_VESSEL_DIR           1  255  "Vessel definitions directory"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRR_BOTTLE_WORKSPACE     1   64  "Container workspace path for bottle working directory"
  buv_ipv4_enroll    RBRR_DNS_SERVER                   "DNS server for containers"

  buv_group_enroll "GCP Infrastructure"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER            1   26  "Depot moniker — paired with CLOUD_PREFIX to derive depot project ID, GAR repo, pool stem, and bucket"
  buv_gname_enroll   RBRR_GCP_REGION               1   32  "GCP region"

  buv_group_enroll "Google Cloud Build Configuration"
  buv_gname_enroll   RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE           3   64  "Machine type for Cloud Build (CE format)"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRR_GCB_TIMEOUT                2   10  "Build timeout (e.g., 1200s)"
  buv_decimal_enroll RBRR_GCB_MIN_CONCURRENT_BUILDS  1  999  "Min concurrent builds required"

  buv_group_enroll "Secrets Directory"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRR_SECRETS_DIR              1  512  "Directory containing credential files"

  buv_group_enroll "Public Docs"
  buv_string_enroll  RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL          1  512  "Public docs URL — readme target for buh_tlt glossary links, updated per-release or per-incorporation"

  # Guard against unexpected RBRR_ variables not in enrollment
  buv_scope_sentinel RBRR RBRR_

  # Build docker env args array from validated values
  # Usage: docker run "${ZRBRR_DOCKER_ENV[@]}" ...
  ZRBRR_DOCKER_ENV=("-e" "RBRR_DNS_SERVER=${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}")
  readonly ZRBRR_DOCKER_ENV

  # Lock all enrolled RBRR_ variables against mutation
  buv_lock RBRR

  readonly ZRBRR_KINDLED=1
}

zrbrr_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBRR_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbrr not kindled - call zrbrr_kindle first"
}

# Enforce all RBRR enrollment validations and custom format checks
zrbrr_enforce() {
  zrbrr_sentinel

  buv_vet RBRR

  buv_dir_exists "${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}"
  buv_dir_exists "${RBRR_SECRETS_DIR}"

  [[ "${RBRR_GCB_TIMEOUT}" =~ ^[0-9]+s$ ]] \
    || buc_die "Invalid RBRR_GCB_TIMEOUT format: ${RBRR_GCB_TIMEOUT} (expected NNNs)"

  [[ "${RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER}" =~ ^[a-z][a-z0-9]*$ ]] \
    || buc_die "Invalid RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER format: ${RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER} (expected lowercase alphanumeric starting with letter; no hyphens)"

  [[ "${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}"   =~ ^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*-$ ]] \
    || buc_die "Invalid RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX format: ${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}   (expected lowercase starting with letter, ending in hyphen)"

  [[ "${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX}" =~ ^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*-$ ]] \
    || buc_die "Invalid RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX format: ${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX} (expected lowercase starting with letter, ending in hyphen)"

  # Joint-length cap: GCP project IDs max 30 chars. RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID
  # composes as "${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}d-${RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER}".
  #
  # Test fixtures (rbtdrp_pristine, rbtdrk_canonical) compose BURS_TINCTURE
  # (1-3 chars) into BOTH the prefix and the moniker before writing rbrr.env
  # for parallel-station disjointness. The cap below applies to whatever is
  # in rbrr.env at validation time — production-authored or fixture-written.
  # Fixture bases are short (≤7 chars) so tinctured fixture values stay well
  # under the cap; the joint check exists to catch operator-authored prefix
  # and moniker pairs that would overflow GCP's project-ID limit.
  local -r z_prefix_len=${#RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}
  local -r z_moniker_len=${#RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER}
  local -r z_tincture_len=${#BURS_TINCTURE}
  local -r z_joint_len=$(( z_prefix_len + 2 + z_moniker_len ))
  test "${z_joint_len}" -le 30 \
    || buc_die "Joint length exceeds 30-char GCP project ID limit: RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX(${z_prefix_len}) + 'd-'(2) + RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER(${z_moniker_len}) = ${z_joint_len} (BURS_TINCTURE=${BURS_TINCTURE} contributes ${z_tincture_len} chars to each term when fixtures write)"

}

# eof

file.content:
readonly RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE="${RBRR_SECRETS_DIR}/${RBCC_role_retriever}/${RBCC_rbra_file}"
  readonly RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE="${RBRR_SECRETS_DIR}/${RBCC_role_director}/${RBCC_rbra_file}"
  readonly RBDC_PAYOR_RBRO_FILE="${RBRR_SECRETS_DIR}/${RBCC_role_payor}/${RBCC_rbro_file}"
  readonly RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE="${RBRR_SECRETS_DIR}/${RBCC_role_assay}/${RBCC_rbra_file}"

  # Derive depot identity from (RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX, RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER).
  # Project ID, GAR repository, pool stem, and bucket fall out at kindle.
  # Project-ID infix lifted to RBGC tinder (`RBGC_depot_project_infix`),
  # available at source time without rbgc kindle ordering dependency.
  readonly RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID="${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}${RBGC_depot_project_infix}${RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER}"
  readonly RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY="${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}${RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER}-gar"
  readonly RBDC_GCB_POOL_STEM="${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}${RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER}-pool"
  readonly RBDC_GCS_BUCKET="${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}b-${RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER}"

  # Derive full pool resource paths from stem (suffixes match RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_TETHER/AIRGAP)
  readonly RBDC_POOL_TETHER="projects/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/locations/${RBRR_GCP_REGION}/workerPools/${RBDC_GCB_POOL_STEM}-tether"
  readonly RBDC_POOL_AIRGAP="projects/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/locations/${RBRR_GCP_REGION}/workerPools/${RBDC_GCB_POOL_STEM}-airgap"

  readonly ZRBDC_KINDLED=1
}

zrbdc_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBDC_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbdc not kindled - call zrbdc_kindle first"
}

content.description: rbtdrp_pristine.rs: thread tincture into THROWAWAY_CLOUD_PREFIX, THROWAWAY_RUNTIME_PREFIX, FAMILY_STEM_ARC composition for case-time disjointness across stations.

content.description: rbtdrk_canonical.rs: thread tincture into CANONICAL_CLOUD_PREFIX, CANONICAL_RUNTIME_PREFIX, FAMILY_STEM composition for canonical depot disjointness across stations.

content.subject: Update rbrr_regime.sh joint-length validator
content.description: Account for BURS_TINCTURE budget impact in GCP project-ID joint-length cap. Specific shape TBD after surveying composition pattern.

content.description: Render fixture-composed names with two distinct BURS_TINCTURE values; confirm disjoint depot IDs, GAR repos, SA emails.

content: convince me you have a good plan for this verification dry-run

input.command: shellcheck -S error Tools/buk/burs_regime.sh Tools/rbk/rbrr_regime.sh && echo "shellcheck clean"

content.content: shellcheck clean

toolUseResult.stdout: shellcheck clean

attachment.prompt: convince me you have a good plan for this verification dry-run

content.content: Updated task #5 status

content.text:
Honest answer: the plan I built is decent unit-test coverage, but probably not what the wrap criterion is asking for. Let me lay out what I have versus what would actually convince an operator.

## What I built

Two Rust unit tests — `rbtdtp_pristine_dual_station_disjoint` and `rbtdtk_canonical_dual_station_disjoint`. Each:

1. Picks two synthetic tinctures `"aaa"` and `"bbb"`
2. Calls the composer fns: `rbtdrp_throwaway_cloud_prefix(t)`, `rbtdrp_family_stem_arc(t)`, etc.
3. **Recapitulates RBDC's derivation rules inline** as `format!("{}d-{}", cloud, moniker)`
4. Asserts disjoint project_id / GAR repo / bucket / SA email
5. Asserts project_id ≤ 30 chars

## What this actually proves

- The composer functions return distinct strings for distinct inputs. (Almost trivial — `format!("{}{}-", base, t)` can't produce equal strings for distinct `t`.)

## What it does NOT prove

1. **RBDC drift**: the test inlines `format!("{}d-{}", cloud, moniker)` — if `rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh` line 84 changes its composition, my test happily passes while production breaks. Production reads RBDC; my test does not.

2. **Install path**: `rbtdrp_install_throwaway_prefixes` and `rbtdrk_install_canonical_prefixes` are the actual write functions — they read BURD_STATION_FILE, parse BURS_TINCTURE, compose, write rbrr.env, commit. None of that is exercised at dual-station scale. I trust the composer transitively.

3. **Moniker picker**: hardcoded suffix `"100000"`. The real `rbtdrp_pick_next_moniker` walks fact files; cross-station fact-file disjointness (per-stem prefix-strip) isn't exercised.

4. **End-to-end station-file path**: my test bypasses `BURD_STATION_FILE → parse → tincture`. If that wiring breaks, my test still passes.

## What would actually convince me

A bash dry-run tabtarget that, on a single machine:

1. Writes a temp burs.env with `BURS_TINCTURE=aaa`, points BURD_STATION_FILE at it, runs `rbtdrp_install_throwaway_prefixes` against a tempdir rbrr.env, then kindles RBDC and emits all derived values
2. Repeats with `BURS_TINCTURE=bbb` against a separate tempdir
3. Diffs the two emitted manifests, asserts disjointness on the actual RBDC outputs
4. No git commits, no GCP calls

This exercises the real composition path through real RBDC kindle. It's the test that would have caught my "RBDC composition drift" hole.

## Recommendation

Either accept the unit tests as the wrap-criterion bar — they meet the literal "shows disjoint" requirement for the items named (project IDs, GAR repos, SA emails) — and leave the stronger path as a follow-up itch. Or stretch the pace and build the bash dry-run.

I lean toward a small bash dry-run before wrap. The Rust tests stay (they're cheap and they assert the composer contract); the bash adds the integration-shape coverage. Maybe 30 minutes of work.

Which way?

message.content: I'm actually inclned to conclude this feature development as is, but then to reslate the two paces for macos/linux running of the whole suite to be aware of each other in the event of collisions.  Thoughts?

content.text:
₢BBABB
₢BBAAX
₢BBABA
₢BBAA8
₢BBAAH
₢BBAAI
₢BBAAq
₢BBAAt
₢BBAAw
₢BBAAx
₢BBAAy
₢BBAA1
₢BBAA5

I agree. Two reasons it's better than building the bash dry-run now:

1. **The downstream paces hit the real path anyway.** They run actual GCP calls through real RBDC kindle. A collision-aware version of those paces would catch composition bugs (RBDC drift, fixture install path, station-file wiring) more thoroughly than my synthetic dry-run, because it's the production path under load.

2. **You were going to reslate them anyway.** The current BBABB docket's out-of-scope already says "Reslating BBABA's docket — its parallel-runs claim becomes accurate once this lands." Adding collision-awareness to that already-pending reslate is one edit, not two.

Let me find the two paces and confirm the names.

## Character

Validate BBAAX's repairs (charge auto-summon + ordain rmi removals) by re-running rbw-tP end-to-end on a branch named after this pace's coronet. Branch isolation lets parallel attempts (e.g., simultaneous macOS + Linux runs) avoid the commit-trail race on main.

## Wrap criterion

Green tally end-to-end. Same guardrail as BBAAX — partial completion is not pass.

## Cost

~90m wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt.

## Scope

- Create a branch named after this pace's coronet, off current main
- Run rbw-tP from marshal-zero on that branch

content.content:
**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.

content.text:
Looking at the remaining paces in heat BB, I see only one parallel-runs pace: ₢BBABA (rbw-tP-branched-rerun). Its docket says "Operator selects which machine the pace mounts on (mac or linux); pace does NOT use foray."

So when you say "the two paces for macos/linux", I want to confirm the shape:

**Option 1 — Split BBABA.** Slate a sibling pace so BBABA becomes BBABA-mac + a new pace for linux, each runnable concurrently. Both dockets gain collision-aware behavior.

**Option 2 — Keep BBABA singular but make it collision-aware.** It stays one pace, run twice (operator-driven), and the docket commits to behaviors like "log composed depot_project_id / GAR / SA emails before any GCP call; if a sibling station's manifest is observable, diff and refuse on overlap."

**Option 3 — I'm missing a second pace.** You have a pace in mind I haven't found.

For collision-aware behavior, I'd lean toward language like:
- Each station emits a per-station manifest of all RBDC-derived names (project_id, GAR repo, bucket, SA emails) at first usable point, BEFORE any GCP call
- If a peer's manifest is reachable (shared filesystem, payor manor metadata, whatever's natural), the pace diffs and refuses on any overlap with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the colliding term
- Mid-run "this looks like a tincture composition collision" gets distinguished from "transient GCP race"

Which option, and is that the right shape for "collision-aware"?

content.text:
Disk survey (threshold 85%): 228 GB: 82%, 228 GB: 82%
Heat: rbk-mvp-3-release-qualification (₣BB)
Status: racing
Created: 260426


## Paces

### burs-tincture-station-namespacing (₢BBABB) [rough]

## Character

Add BURS_TINCTURE per-station prefix to the BURS schema. Test fixtures compose it into every cloud-side name they write so parallel rbw-tP runs from different stations land in disjoint GCP projects on a shared payor manor.

## Wrap criterion

Test-side dual-station dry-run (two distinct BURS_TINCTURE values) shows disjoint depot project IDs, GAR repos, and SA emails before any GCP call would race. Actual parallel mac+linux validation is BBABA's job.

## Scope

- Enroll BURS_TINCTURE in burs_regime.sh alongside BURS_USER and BURS_LOG_DIR
- Compose into test-fixture writes: pristine throwaway cloud/runtime prefixes, canonical family stem, any RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX/RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX value the fixtures install into rbrr.env
- Production code unchanged — it reads rbrr.env and uses whatever the test wrote
- Update the joint-length validator (rbrr_regime.sh GCP project-ID budget) to account for the new term

## Out of scope

- Production-side BURS_TINCTURE consumers — test-only by intent
- Director/Retriever identity prefixing — redundant once depots are disjoint
- BBABA's actual parallel mac+linux execution — separate pace
- Reslating BBABA's docket — its parallel-runs claim becomes accurate once this lands

## Constraints

- Test-only scope is load-bearing; production behavior unchanged
- Default semantics for unset BURS_TINCTURE (empty vs required) decided at mount time

### rbw-tP-first-run (₢BBAAX) [rough]

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.

Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
- Triage routing per surprise:
  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Constraints

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
- Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
- Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
- Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue

## Cross-pace dependencies

- gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)

## Out of scope

- Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
- /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
- Multi-day re-run scenarios
- Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

### rbw-tP-branched-rerun (₢BBABA) [rough]

## Character

Validate BBAAX's repairs (charge auto-summon + ordain rmi removals) by re-running rbw-tP end-to-end on a branch named after this pace's coronet. Branch isolation lets parallel attempts (e.g., simultaneous macOS + Linux runs) avoid the commit-trail race on main.

## Wrap criterion

Green tally end-to-end. Same guardrail as BBAAX — partial completion is not pass.

## Cost

~90m wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt.

## Scope

- Create a branch named after this pace's coronet, off current main
- Run rbw-tP from marshal-zero on that branch
- Operator selects which machine the pace mounts on (mac or linux); pace does NOT use foray
- Triage surprises per BBAAX rules: tiny inline fix + re-run, larger → follow-up pace

## Locked decisions

- Branch name == pace coronet (identity-stable; no human-meaningful name)
- Branch lives until pace wraps; deletion is operator territory

### docker-login-retry-shape (₢BBAA8) [rough]

## Character

Design conversation requiring judgment. Mechanism choice must follow operator concurrence — load-bearing slate-time instruction.

## Issue evidence

Failed in BBAAX first-run gauntlet attempt at `rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_conjure` verification tail. Conjure cloud build (4m0s) and vouch (1m30s) both succeeded. All GAR artifacts confirmed via gcloud post-failure (image, attest-amd64, attest-arm64, about, vouch tagged correctly). Failure was wrest's `docker login` against `us-central1-docker.pkg.dev`:

    Error response from daemon: Get "https://us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/v2/":
    net/http: request canceled while waiting for connection
    (Client.Timeout exceeded while awaiting headers)

Endpoint probed clean post-failure (curl 134ms, 401 as expected; gcloud listing fast). Documented docker daemon transient per moby/moby#44350: non-configurable Client.Timeout fires before the registry auth backend responds; industry remediation across the corpus is retry. Same case had 4 curl rc=28 warnings during the preceding vouch poll, absorbed by the existing 3-consecutive tolerance.

## Asymmetry to close

`Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` poll loop tolerates this transient class with a 3-consecutive policy (BCG-clean, in production).
`Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` `rbfl_wrest`'s docker login is single-shot, fatal on the same transient class. Same daemon, same registry boundary, different fate.

## Scope

Retry shape for transient-vulnerable docker daemon ↔ registry single-shot calls. Immediate surface is wrest's docker login; design surface should include other single-shot daemon calls crossing the same boundary (login, pull, push, inspect with registry round-trip).

## Discuss before implementing — load-bearing

Mount agent surfaces the design space and proposes a recommended shape; operator concurrence precedes landing. No pre-baking at slate-time. Design questions to seat at the table belong in the conversation, not the docket.

## Out of scope

- Curl/gcloud transient audit beyond the docker daemon path — distinct class
- Build-poll loop changes (already tolerant)
- Generic retry framework — only what the surveyed surface requires

## References

- moby/moby#44350 — non-configurable docker daemon Client.Timeout
- BBAAX inline-fix commits 24e24651 (depot Cloud Build URL) and dd5893ba (Mid-execution posture rule) — first-run iteration arc context

### prep-release-integration (₢BBAAH) [rough]

## Character

Final integration pace. Wires pristine-pass as a precondition for `/rbk-prep-release` so the upstream contribution ceremony cannot proceed without a recent pristine green. Closes the loop: pristine qualification becomes load-bearing on release prep, not optional ceremony.

## Work

Modify `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` to add a precondition step: verify rbw-tP ran green against the current HEAD. If not, prep-release fails-fast with operator-visible message naming the marshal-zero → pristine sequence.

Recency contract is **exact-HEAD-match** — strongest invariant, prevents stale passes. rbw-tP green tally writes an artifact linking pass to HEAD's commit SHA; `/rbk-prep-release` reads it and refuses on mismatch. Artifact is gitignored (deliberately not committed). Artifact mechanism (file location, contents) is a mount-time decision.

Update README "Release Procedure" section to reflect that the prep-release step now enforces rbw-tP had to pass against the very HEAD that prep-release is preparing. README references the post-success cleanup ceremony documented in RELEASE.md (owned by BBAAI).

## Verification

- `/rbk-prep-release` fails fast when no pristine pass is recorded for current HEAD
- After successful rbw-tP, `/rbk-prep-release` proceeds normally
- After successful rbw-tP followed by an unrelated commit, `/rbk-prep-release` refuses (SHA mismatch)
- Pristine-passed artifact is gitignored
- README precondition documentation references the cleanup ceremony in RELEASE.md

## Out of scope

- Cleanup ceremony documentation in RELEASE.md (owned by BBAAI)
- Restructuring the rest of `/rbk-prep-release` (preserved as-is)
- Multi-pristine-run aggregation
- CI integration
- Recency policies beyond exact-HEAD-match (other policies left for future heats if needed)

### release-doc-revision (₢BBAAI) [rough]

## Character

Maintenance pace — re-read RELEASE.md with fresh eyes after the rest of the heat lands and apply accumulated fixes the operator identified during heat work but did not address inline. Material gauntlet-design impact: vocabulary, structure, and post-success ceremony all need RELEASE.md updates beyond simple cleanup.

Owns post-success cleanup ceremony placement in RELEASE.md; BBAAH wires the precondition and references this section from README.

## Character

Reflective audit pace at the tail of BB. Walk the changes landed across BB's paces and determine which RBS0 (and adjacent veiled-spec) sections fell out of sync with code/behavior, then update them.

Cognitive posture: deliberate read-and-compare. Source of truth is the code/handbook surface as it stands at mount-time; RBS0 is being reconciled to it.

## Wrap criterion

RBS0 (and any directly-implicated sibling spec files under `rbk/vov_veiled/`) reflect the behavior shipped during BB. Each touched section either updated or explicitly judged still-correct. No silent drift left behind for the next heat.

## Constraints

## Out of scope

- Restructuring RBS0 beyond what BB's changes require.
- Spec touches outside the veiled-spec family unless directly implicated.

## Known sync points (running list — append as paces land)

- **`RBSAJ-access_jwt_probe.adoc`** (from BBAAc inline fix on the JWT-SA probe — IAM-policy-read propagation race absorption). The procedural spec describes the OLD semantic: "all iterations must pass; HTTP 403 → fatal access denied; HTTP 401 → fatal token rejected." New code semantic in `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`: bounded propagation-absorbing retry — first 2xx wins, HTTP 403 retries within `max_attempts × delay_ms` budget, all other codes (including 401) fall to a single fail-fast catch-all with body-summary diagnostic via `zrbgv_jq_error_message_capture`. Sections needing rewrite: §"Execute Probe Iterations" (rename concept to *attempts*; rewrite "Evaluate Response" sub-section to reflect 2xx-wins / 403-retry / else-fatal); §"Display Summary" ("succeeded after «I» attempt(s)" instead of "«N»/«N» iterations passed"); the top NOTE block (intent shift from sustained-access verification to propagation-window absorption + verification); §"Validate Input Parameters" (`ITERATION_COUNT` description shifts to "max attempts — first 2xx wins"; `DELAY_MS` to "between retries"). Companion call-site change: `rbte_engine.sh::rbte_probe` defaults shifted to 60 × 3000ms (≈180s budget) for SA roles only; Payor probe defaults and semantic unchanged.

### handbook-render-param1-coverage (₢BBAAw) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A-AAS in ₣A-.

## Character
Future follow-up to the handbook-render fixture landed in ₣A6 (₢A6AAt). Design decision pace — likely requires some discussion of how to represent per-colophon parameterization in Theurge's test fixtures before any code moves.

## Docket
The handbook-render fixture in ₣A6 exercises 15 of the 16 handbook colophons. The one skipped is `rbw-HWdw` (`RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE`), which uses the `param1` channel — it requires a WSL-target argument to run, and the fixture's uniform "invoke and check exit 0" pattern does not supply one.

Decide how the handbook-render fixture should handle this (and any future param1-channel handbooks). Candidate directions:

- **Per-colophon argument lookup**: extend the Rust-side handbook colophon registry with a `default_args` slice per entry. Most entries get `&[]`; `rbw-HWdw` gets a known-safe dummy. Keeps the case loop uniform, localizes the exception.
- **Dedicated subsection in the fixture**: a second section listing only param1 handbooks with their dummy values. Preserves the "one case per colophon" symmetry without threading argument tables through the uniform case.
- **Split fixture**: `handbook-render-simple` (no args) + `handbook-render-parameterized` (args explicit). Heavier and probably not worth it for one offender.

This pace picks a direction, implements it, and extends handbook-render coverage to `rbw-HWdw`.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAt (handbook-render-fixture) must be complete — this pace patches a known scope deferral from that work.

### Wrap criterion
- handbook-render fixture exercises all 16 handbook colophons (including `rbw-HWdw`)
- Pattern generalizes to any future param1 handbooks added via `rbz_zipper.sh`
- Test asserts the chosen dummy param1 value(s) produce a rendering exit (not an argument-missing exit)

### Notes
- Known exit 0 is not the only valid verdict here. The test's purpose is to catch display breakage in handbook rendering — the dummy argument must steer toward a rendered output path, not an early-exit-due-to-bad-arg path.
- `rbw-HWdw` is currently the only param1 handbook. If a second one lands before this pace runs, reassess whether an inline table is still the right shape.

### handbook-render-imprint-coverage (₢BBAAx) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A-AAT in ₣A-.

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

### rbtd-fixture-nomenclature-collapse (₢BBAAy) [rough]

## Character

Two-step nomenclature simplification in the rbtd fixture engine.
Mechanical refactor with one careful read of test-coverage impact.

## Goals

1. Eliminate "case name string" as a parallel surface to function name.
   Today `case!()` already sets name from `stringify!($func)`, so case
   names ARE function names — but operator-facing surfaces (commit
   prose, dockets, error messages) treat the kebab form as if it were
   a key. Make canonical = function name; either drop kebab from
   user-facing output entirely, or accept kebab as an alias resolver.

2. Eliminate `rbtdre_Section` abstraction. Replace with flat
   `cases: &[Case]` directly on `rbtdre_Fixture`. Fixture-level
   fail-fast vs keep-going (already controlled by disposition) is the
   only granularity actually wanted. Multi-section fixtures
   (enrollment-validation, regime-validation) absorb their grouping
   into case-name prefixes if helpful for display.

## Wrap criterion

- All sections collapsed; `rbtdre_Section` struct removed.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` inter-section coverage retired or repurposed
  (it currently exists to test the abstraction being removed).
- All fixtures still pass under their existing tabtargets.

## Out of scope

- Renaming individual case functions for prose readability.
- Changes to fixture lifecycle (setup/teardown), dispositions,
  or run/list tabtarget surfaces.
- Anything in rbtw or upstream of the engine API.

### single-case-imprint-collapse (₢BBAA1) [rough]

## Character

Tabtarget-surface refactor. Mechanical except for one judgment call:
the colophon name and channel.

## Goals

Replace the imprint-channel `rbtd-s.FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh [case]`
pattern with a single non-imprint, two-param colophon: param1 is the
fixture name, param2 is the rust test case function name (e.g.
`rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels`). The new tabtarget covers ALL
manifest-registered fixtures, not just crucible ones.

The tabtarget is self-documenting — invocation with missing or invalid
arguments fails with a usage listing instead of an opaque error:

- No params: list all valid param1 values (fixture names from the
  manifest); exit nonzero.
- Invalid param1: same — list all valid param1 values; exit nonzero.
- Valid param1, missing or invalid param2: list valid param2 values
  (case names registered for that fixture); exit nonzero.
- Both valid: run the case.

## Wrap criterion

- Single new tabtarget routes by fixture-name lookup against the
  rbtd manifest; rejects unknown fixture names.
- Helpful-failure UX: missing/invalid args produce a list of valid
  values for the offending position and a nonzero exit.
- Per-fixture FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh files retired.
- Crucible-fixture single-case semantics preserved: still operates on
  already-charged state, never charges/quenches.
- BUK channel discipline honored — the new colophon enrolls under the
  param1 channel, not imprint; zipper updated; CLAUDE.md tabtarget table
  reflects the change.
- Fast suite green; canonical onboarding-sequence cases unchanged.

## Out of scope

- FixtureRun (whole-fixture) tabtargets — unchanged.
- Charge/quench tabtargets — unchanged.
- Adding new fixtures or new manifest fields.

## References

- Tools/buk/buk-claude-context.md — channel semantics (imprint vs param1)
- BBAAz onboarding-sequence-buildout pattern — precedent for
  manifest-driven dispatch
- rbtd manifest: rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs

### http-helper-callsite-port (₢BBAA5) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical DRY port: replace inline curl-with-stderr-capture blocks across the codebase with calls to `rbgu_http_request` (helper landed in BBAAs, lives in `Tools/rbk/rbgu_Utility.sh`). Mount-time discovery — do not assume the surface is what it was when this was slated.

## Discovery recipe

At mount, scan the curl callsites:

```
grep -n 'curl' Tools/rbk/*.sh | grep -v 'rbgu_http_request\|--head\|rbgj'
```

For each hit, decide: compatible or not?

A callsite is **compatible** with the helper when its curl shape matches all of:
- Method-parameterized HTTP request (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE)
- Bearer-token auth passed via a separate token variable
- Accept and Content-Type headers (when present) are `application/json`
- Optional JSON body file via `-d @body`
- Caller wants response body, HTTP status code, and stderr captured to files (rather than discarded or piped)

A callsite is **incompatible** (leave inline; possibly sibling-helper in a future pass) when any of:
- HEAD-only existence probe (`--head`)
- Form-encoded body (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`)
- Runs in a different shell environment (e.g., alpine sh inside Cloud Build step scripts)
- Other Content-Type or unusual header set the helper doesn't speak

## Wrap criterion

- Every callsite found by the recipe has been classified compatible or incompatible
- Every compatible callsite calls the helper instead of inlining the curl flags
- No new inline curl block matching the compatible shape has appeared in the working tree
- `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` green; `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` baseline preserved
- At least one empirical run exercises a ported callsite end-to-end

## Cross-pace dependencies

- BBAAs landed (helper available)

## Out of scope

- Tolerance/timeout tuning of any retry or polling loop
- New helper variants (form-encoded, HEAD-predicate, etc.) — those are separate paces if warranted
- Restructuring caller-side retry/policy logic — only the curl-flag block is portable; the loop around it stays as-is

## Paces

### burs-tincture-station-namespacing (₢BBABB) [rough]

## Character

Add BURS_TINCTURE per-station prefix to the BURS schema. Test fixtures compose it into every cloud-side name they write so parallel rbw-tP runs from different stations land in disjoint GCP projects on a shared payor manor.

## Wrap criterion

Test-side dual-station dry-run (two distinct BURS_TINCTURE values) shows disjoint depot project IDs, GAR repos, and SA emails before any GCP call would race. Actual parallel mac+linux validation is BBABA's job.

## Scope

- Enroll BURS_TINCTURE in burs_regime.sh alongside BURS_USER and BURS_LOG_DIR
- Compose into test-fixture writes: pristine throwaway cloud/runtime prefixes, canonical family stem, any RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX/RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX value the fixtures install into rbrr.env
- Production code unchanged — it reads rbrr.env and uses whatever the test wrote
- Update the joint-length validator (rbrr_regime.sh GCP project-ID budget) to account for the new term

## Out of scope

- Production-side BURS_TINCTURE consumers — test-only by intent
- Director/Retriever identity prefixing — redundant once depots are disjoint
- BBABA's actual parallel mac+linux execution — separate pace
- Reslating BBABA's docket — its parallel-runs claim becomes accurate once this lands

## Constraints

- Test-only scope is load-bearing; production behavior unchanged
- Default semantics for unset BURS_TINCTURE (empty vs required) decided at mount time

### rbw-tP-first-run (₢BBAAX) [rough]

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.

Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
- Triage routing per surprise:
  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Constraints

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
- Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
- Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
- Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue

## Cross-pace dependencies

- gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)

## Out of scope

- Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
- /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
- Multi-day re-run scenarios
- Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

### rbw-tP-branched-rerun (₢BBABA) [rough]

## Character

Validate BBAAX's repairs (charge auto-summon + ordain rmi removals) by re-running rbw-tP end-to-end on a branch named after this pace's coronet. Branch isolation lets parallel attempts (e.g., simultaneous macOS + Linux runs) avoid the commit-trail race on main.

## Wrap criterion

Green tally end-to-end. Same guardrail as BBAAX — partial completion is not pass.

## Cost

~90m wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt.

## Scope

- Run rbw-tP from marshal-zero on that branch
- Operator selects which machine the pace mounts on (mac or linux); pace does NOT use foray
- Triage surprises per BBAAX rules: tiny inline fix + re-run, larger → follow-up pace

## Locked decisions

- Branch lives until pace wraps; deletion is operator territory

### docker-login-retry-shape (₢BBAA8) [rough]

## Character

Design conversation requiring judgment. Mechanism choice must follow operator concurrence — load-bearing slate-time instruction.

## Issue evidence

Failed in BBAAX first-run gauntlet attempt at `rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_conjure` verification tail. Conjure cloud build (4m0s) and vouch (1m30s) both succeeded. All GAR artifacts confirmed via gcloud post-failure (image, attest-amd64, attest-arm64, about, vouch tagged correctly). Failure was wrest's `docker login` against `us-central1-docker.pkg.dev`:

    Error response from daemon: Get "https://us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/v2/":
    net/http: request canceled while waiting for connection
    (Client.Timeout exceeded while awaiting headers)

Endpoint probed clean post-failure (curl 134ms, 401 as expected; gcloud listing fast). Documented docker daemon transient per moby/moby#44350: non-configurable Client.Timeout fires before the registry auth backend responds; industry remediation across the corpus is retry. Same case had 4 curl rc=28 warnings during the preceding vouch poll, absorbed by the existing 3-consecutive tolerance.

## Asymmetry to close

`Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` poll loop tolerates this transient class with a 3-consecutive policy (BCG-clean, in production).
`Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` `rbfl_wrest`'s docker login is single-shot, fatal on the same transient class. Same daemon, same registry boundary, different fate.

## Scope

Retry shape for transient-vulnerable docker daemon ↔ registry single-shot calls. Immediate surface is wrest's docker login; design surface should include other single-shot daemon calls crossing the same boundary (login, pull, push, inspect with registry round-trip).

## Discuss before implementing — load-bearing

Mount agent surfaces the design space and proposes a recommended shape; operator concurrence precedes landing. No pre-baking at slate-time. Design questions to seat at the table belong in the conversation, not the docket.

## Out of scope

- Curl/gcloud transient audit beyond the docker daemon path — distinct class
- Build-poll loop changes (already tolerant)
- Generic retry framework — only what the surveyed surface requires

## References

- moby/moby#44350 — non-configurable docker daemon Client.Timeout
- BBAAX inline-fix commits 24e24651 (depot Cloud Build URL) and dd5893ba (Mid-execution posture rule) — first-run iteration arc context

### prep-release-integration (₢BBAAH) [rough]

## Character

Final integration pace. Wires pristine-pass as a precondition for `/rbk-prep-release` so the upstream contribution ceremony cannot proceed without a recent pristine green. Closes the loop: pristine qualification becomes load-bearing on release prep, not optional ceremony.

## Work

Modify `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` to add a precondition step: verify rbw-tP ran green against the current HEAD. If not, prep-release fails-fast with operator-visible message naming the marshal-zero → pristine sequence.

Recency contract is **exact-HEAD-match** — strongest invariant, prevents stale passes. rbw-tP green tally writes an artifact linking pass to HEAD's commit SHA; `/rbk-prep-release` reads it and refuses on mismatch. Artifact is gitignored (deliberately not committed). Artifact mechanism (file location, contents) is a mount-time decision.

Update README "Release Procedure" section to reflect that the prep-release step now enforces rbw-tP had to pass against the very HEAD that prep-release is preparing. README references the post-success cleanup ceremony documented in RELEASE.md (owned by BBAAI).

## Verification

- `/rbk-prep-release` fails fast when no pristine pass is recorded for current HEAD
- After successful rbw-tP, `/rbk-prep-release` proceeds normally
- After successful rbw-tP followed by an unrelated commit, `/rbk-prep-release` refuses (SHA mismatch)
- Pristine-passed artifact is gitignored
- README precondition documentation references the cleanup ceremony in RELEASE.md

## Out of scope

- Cleanup ceremony documentation in RELEASE.md (owned by BBAAI)
- Restructuring the rest of `/rbk-prep-release` (preserved as-is)
- Multi-pristine-run aggregation
- CI integration
- Recency policies beyond exact-HEAD-match (other policies left for future heats if needed)

### rbs0-sync-sweep-from-bb-changes (₢BBAAq) [rough]

## Character

Reflective audit pace at the tail of BB. Walk the changes landed across BB's paces and determine which RBS0 (and adjacent veiled-spec) sections fell out of sync with code/behavior, then update them.

Cognitive posture: deliberate read-and-compare. Source of truth is the code/handbook surface as it stands at mount-time; RBS0 is being reconciled to it.

## Wrap criterion

RBS0 (and any directly-implicated sibling spec files under `rbk/vov_veiled/`) reflect the behavior shipped during BB. Each touched section either updated or explicitly judged still-correct. No silent drift left behind for the next heat.

## Known sync points (running list — append as paces land)

- **`RBSAJ-access_jwt_probe.adoc`** (from BBAAc inline fix on the JWT-SA probe — IAM-policy-read propagation race absorption). The procedural spec describes the OLD semantic: "all iterations must pass; HTTP 403 → fatal access denied; HTTP 401 → fatal token rejected." New code semantic in `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`: bounded propagation-absorbing retry — first 2xx wins, HTTP 403 retries within `max_attempts × delay_ms` budget, all other codes (including 401) fall to a single fail-fast catch-all with body-summary diagnostic via `zrbgv_jq_error_message_capture`. Sections needing rewrite: §"Execute Probe Iterations" (rename concept to *attempts*; rewrite "Evaluate Response" sub-section to reflect 2xx-wins / 403-retry / else-fatal); §"Display Summary" ("succeeded after «I» attempt(s)" instead of "«N»/«N» iterations passed"); the top NOTE block (intent shift from sustained-access verification to propagation-window absorption + verification); §"Validate Input Parameters" (`ITERATION_COUNT` description shifts to "max attempts — first 2xx wins"; `DELAY_MS` to "between retries"). Companion call-site change: `rbte_engine.sh::rbte_probe` defaults shifted to 60 × 3000ms (≈180s budget) for SA roles only; Payor probe defaults and semantic unchanged.

### handbook-render-param1-coverage (₢BBAAw) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A-AAS in ₣A-.

## Character
Future follow-up to the handbook-render fixture landed in ₣A6 (₢A6AAt). Design decision pace — likely requires some discussion of how to represent per-colophon parameterization in Theurge's test fixtures before any code moves.

## Docket
The handbook-render fixture in ₣A6 exercises 15 of the 16 handbook colophons. The one skipped is `rbw-HWdw` (`RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE`), which uses the `param1` channel — it requires a WSL-target argument to run, and the fixture's uniform "invoke and check exit 0" pattern does not supply one.

Decide how the handbook-render fixture should handle this (and any future param1-channel handbooks). Candidate directions:

- **Per-colophon argument lookup**: extend the Rust-side handbook colophon registry with a `default_args` slice per entry. Most entries get `&[]`; `rbw-HWdw` gets a known-safe dummy. Keeps the case loop uniform, localizes the exception.
- **Dedicated subsection in the fixture**: a second section listing only param1 handbooks with their dummy values. Preserves the "one case per colophon" symmetry without threading argument tables through the uniform case.
- **Split fixture**: `handbook-render-simple` (no args) + `handbook-render-parameterized` (args explicit). Heavier and probably not worth it for one offender.

This pace picks a direction, implements it, and extends handbook-render coverage to `rbw-HWdw`.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAt (handbook-render-fixture) must be complete — this pace patches a known scope deferral from that work.

### Wrap criterion
- handbook-render fixture exercises all 16 handbook colophons (including `rbw-HWdw`)
- Pattern generalizes to any future param1 handbooks added via `rbz_zipper.sh`
- Test asserts the chosen dummy param1 value(s) produce a rendering exit (not an argument-missing exit)

### Notes
- Known exit 0 is not the only valid verdict here. The test's purpose is to catch display breakage in handbook rendering — the dummy argument must steer toward a rendered output path, not an early-exit-due-to-bad-arg path.
- `rbw-HWdw` is currently the only param1 handbook. If a second one lands before this pace runs, reassess whether an inline table is still the right shape.

### handbook-render-imprint-coverage (₢BBAAx) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A-AAT in ₣A-.

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

### rbtd-fixture-nomenclature-collapse (₢BBAAy) [rough]

## Character

Two-step nomenclature simplification in the rbtd fixture engine.
Mechanical refactor with one careful read of test-coverage impact.

## Goals

1. Eliminate "case name string" as a parallel surface to function name.
   Today `case!()` already sets name from `stringify!($func)`, so case
   names ARE function names — but operator-facing surfaces (commit
   prose, dockets, error messages) treat the kebab form as if it were
   a key. Make canonical = function name; either drop kebab from
   user-facing output entirely, or accept kebab as an alias resolver.

2. Eliminate `rbtdre_Section` abstraction. Replace with flat
   `cases: &[Case]` directly on `rbtdre_Fixture`. Fixture-level
   fail-fast vs keep-going (already controlled by disposition) is the
   only granularity actually wanted. Multi-section fixtures
   (enrollment-validation, regime-validation) absorb their grouping
   into case-name prefixes if helpful for display.

## Wrap criterion

- All sections collapsed; `rbtdre_Section` struct removed.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` inter-section coverage retired or repurposed
  (it currently exists to test the abstraction being removed).
- All fixtures still pass under their existing tabtargets.

## Out of scope

- Renaming individual case functions for prose readability.
- Changes to fixture lifecycle (setup/teardown), dispositions,
  or run/list tabtarget surfaces.
- Anything in rbtw or upstream of the engine API.

### single-case-imprint-collapse (₢BBAA1) [rough]

## Character

Tabtarget-surface refactor. Mechanical except for one judgment call:
the colophon name and channel.

## Goals

Replace the imprint-channel `rbtd-s.FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh [case]`
pattern with a single non-imprint, two-param colophon: param1 is the
fixture name, param2 is the rust test case function name (e.g.
`rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels`). The new tabtarget covers ALL
manifest-registered fixtures, not just crucible ones.

The tabtarget is self-documenting — invocation with missing or invalid
arguments fails with a usage listing instead of an opaque error:

- No params: list all valid param1 values (fixture names from the
  manifest); exit nonzero.
- Invalid param1: same — list all valid param1 values; exit nonzero.
- Valid param1, missing or invalid param2: list valid param2 values
  (case names registered for that fixture); exit nonzero.
- Both valid: run the case.

## Wrap criterion

- Single new tabtarget routes by fixture-name lookup against the
  rbtd manifest; rejects unknown fixture names.
- Helpful-failure UX: missing/invalid args produce a list of valid
  values for the offending position and a nonzero exit.
- Per-fixture FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh files retired.
- Crucible-fixture single-case semantics preserved: still operates on
  already-charged state, never charges/quenches.
- BUK channel discipline honored — the new colophon enrolls under the
  param1 channel, not imprint; zipper updated; CLAUDE.md tabtarget table
  reflects the change.
- Fast suite green; canonical onboarding-sequence cases unchanged.

## Out of scope

- FixtureRun (whole-fixture) tabtargets — unchanged.
- Charge/quench tabtargets — unchanged.
- Adding new fixtures or new manifest fields.

## References

- Tools/buk/buk-claude-context.md — channel semantics (imprint vs param1)
- BBAAz onboarding-sequence-buildout pattern — precedent for
  manifest-driven dispatch
- rbtd manifest: rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs

### http-helper-callsite-port (₢BBAA5) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical DRY port: replace inline curl-with-stderr-capture blocks across the codebase with calls to `rbgu_http_request` (helper landed in BBAAs, lives in `Tools/rbk/rbgu_Utility.sh`). Mount-time discovery — do not assume the surface is what it was when this was slated.

## Discovery recipe

At mount, scan the curl callsites:

```
grep -n 'curl' Tools/rbk/*.sh | grep -v 'rbgu_http_request\|--head\|rbgj'
```

For each hit, decide: compatible or not?

A callsite is **compatible** with the helper when its curl shape matches all of:
- Method-parameterized HTTP request (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE)
- Bearer-token auth passed via a separate token variable
- Accept and Content-Type headers (when present) are `application/json`
- Optional JSON body file via `-d @body`
- Caller wants response body, HTTP status code, and stderr captured to files (rather than discarded or piped)

A callsite is **incompatible** (leave inline; possibly sibling-helper in a future pass) when any of:
- HEAD-only existence probe (`--head`)
- Form-encoded body (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`)
- Runs in a different shell environment (e.g., alpine sh inside Cloud Build step scripts)
- Other Content-Type or unusual header set the helper doesn't speak

## Wrap criterion

- Every callsite found by the recipe has been classified compatible or incompatible
- Every compatible callsite calls the helper instead of inlining the curl flags
- No new inline curl block matching the compatible shape has appeared in the working tree
- `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` green; `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` baseline preserved
- At least one empirical run exercises a ported callsite end-to-end

## Cross-pace dependencies

- BBAAs landed (helper available)

## Out of scope

- Tolerance/timeout tuning of any retry or polling loop
- New helper variants (form-encoded, HEAD-predicate, etc.) — those are separate paces if warranted
- Restructuring caller-side retry/policy logic — only the curl-flag block is portable; the loop around it stays as-is

content.text:
Found them. Two paces both run rbw-tP end-to-end across the full suite:

- **₢BBAAX** — `rbw-tP-first-run` — initial gauntlet run; surprises trigger inline fixes or follow-up paces. Its docket says "wrap criterion is green tally."
- **₢BBABA** — `rbw-tP-branched-rerun` — validates BBAAX's repairs on a branch named after the coronet. Docket explicitly says "Operator selects which machine the pace mounts on (mac or linux); pace does NOT use foray. Branch isolation lets parallel attempts (e.g., simultaneous macOS + Linux runs) avoid the commit-trail race on main."

Reading them together: BBAAX is the first run (one machine), BBABA is the validation run (the other machine). Pre-BBABB they could only run serially on a shared payor; post-BBABB they can run in parallel because tincture makes the GCP names disjoint.

The reslate would touch both dockets to:
1. Recognize they can run in parallel post-BBABB (state the dependency)
2. Bake in collision-aware behavior — manifest emission of all RBDC-derived names before any GCP call, sibling-station diff if observable, fail-fast with operator-actionable diagnostic on overlap distinguishing tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race

Is BBAAX + BBABA the pair you remembered? If so, I'll draft the reslate dockets for both.
Drafting the reslate now. Two notices in `gazette_in.md`, one mass `jjx_redocket` call.

input.content:
# jjezs_reslate BBAAX

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.

Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.

Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
- Pre-flight emission: before any GCP call, emit a per-station manifest naming the active BURS_TINCTURE and every RBDC-derived name this run will allocate (depot project_id, GAR repo, GCS bucket, governor/director/retriever SA emails). Manifest format and emission site are mount-time decisions; the load-bearing property is "operator can eyeball the manifest and confirm disjointness from any concurrent peer's manifest before either run touches GCP."
- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
- Triage routing per surprise:
  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Collision-aware diagnostic

Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.

## Constraints

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
- Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
- Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
- Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue

## Cross-pace dependencies

- gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
- BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property and for the pre-flight manifest's tincture line to be meaningful)

## Out of scope

- Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
- /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
- Multi-day re-run scenarios
- Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

# jjezs_reslate BBABA

## Character

Validate BBAAX's repairs by re-running rbw-tP end-to-end on a branch named after this pace's coronet. Branch isolation lets parallel attempts on different machines avoid the commit-trail race on main; with BBABB landed, parallel mac+linux execution against a shared payor manor is now first-class — distinct BURS_TINCTURE values give every RBDC-derived name (depot project_id, GAR repo, GCS bucket, SA emails) per-station disjointness by construction.

## Wrap criterion

Green tally end-to-end. Same guardrail as BBAAX — partial completion is not pass.

## Cost

~90m wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt.

## Scope

- Create a branch named after this pace's coronet, off current main
- Pre-flight emission: before any GCP call, emit a per-station manifest naming the active BURS_TINCTURE and every RBDC-derived name this run will allocate (mirrors BBAAX). Operator may diff this against BBAAX's concurrent manifest to confirm disjointness before either run proceeds.
- Run rbw-tP from marshal-zero on the branch
- Operator selects which machine the pace mounts on (mac or linux); pace does NOT use foray
- Triage surprises per BBAAX rules: tiny inline fix + re-run, larger → follow-up pace

## Collision-aware diagnostic

Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.

## Locked decisions

- Branch name == pace coronet (identity-stable; no human-meaningful name)
- Branch lives until pace wraps; deletion is operator territory

## Cross-pace dependencies

- BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; the parallel-station property and the pre-flight manifest's tincture line both depend on it)

Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
- Pre-flight emission: before any GCP call, emit a per-station manifest naming the active BURS_TINCTURE and every RBDC-derived name this run will allocate (depot project_id, GAR repo, GCS bucket, governor/director/retriever SA emails). Manifest format and emission site are mount-time decisions; the load-bearing property is "operator can eyeball the manifest and confirm disjointness from any concurrent peer's manifest before either run touches GCP."
- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
- Triage routing per surprise:
  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Collision-aware diagnostic

Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.

## Constraints

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
- Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
- Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
- Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue

## Cross-pace dependencies

- gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
- BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property and for the pre-flight manifest's tincture line to be meaningful)

## Out of scope

- Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
- /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
- Multi-day re-run scenarios
- Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

# jjezs_reslate BBABA

## Character

Validate BBAAX's repairs by re-running rbw-tP end-to-end on a branch named after this pace's coronet. Branch isolation lets parallel attempts on different machines avoid the commit-trail race on main; with BBABB landed, parallel mac+linux execution against a shared payor manor is now first-class — distinct BURS_TINCTURE values give every RBDC-derived name (depot project_id, GAR repo, GCS bucket, SA emails) per-station disjointness by construction.

## Wrap criterion

Green tally end-to-end. Same guardrail as BBAAX — partial completion is not pass.

## Cost

~90m wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt.

## Scope

- Create a branch named after this pace's coronet, off current main
- Pre-flight emission: before any GCP call, emit a per-station manifest naming the active BURS_TINCTURE and every RBDC-derived name this run will allocate (mirrors BBAAX). Operator may diff this against BBAAX's concurrent manifest to confirm disjointness before either run proceeds.
- Run rbw-tP from marshal-zero on the branch
- Operator selects which machine the pace mounts on (mac or linux); pace does NOT use foray
- Triage surprises per BBAAX rules: tiny inline fix + re-run, larger → follow-up pace

## Collision-aware diagnostic

Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.

## Locked decisions

- Branch name == pace coronet (identity-stable; no human-meaningful name)
- Branch lives until pace wraps; deletion is operator territory

## Cross-pace dependencies

- BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; the parallel-station property and the pre-flight manifest's tincture line both depend on it)

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Add BURS_TINCTURE per-station prefix to the BURS schema. Test fixtures compose it into every cloud-side name they write so parallel rbw-tP runs from different stations land in disjoint GCP projects on a shared payor manor.
  
  ## Wrap criterion
  
  Test-side dual-station dry-run (two distinct BURS_TINCTURE values) shows disjoint depot project IDs, GAR repos, and SA emails before any GCP call would race. Actual parallel mac+linux validation is BBABA's job.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Enroll BURS_TINCTURE in burs_regime.sh alongside BURS_USER and BURS_LOG_DIR
  - Compose into test-fixture writes: pristine throwaway cloud/runtime prefixes, canonical family stem, any RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX/RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX value the fixtures install into rbrr.env
  - Production code unchanged — it reads rbrr.env and uses whatever the test wrote
  - Update the joint-length validator (rbrr_regime.sh GCP project-ID budget) to account for the new term
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Production-side BURS_TINCTURE consumers — test-only by intent
  - Director/Retriever identity prefixing — redundant once depots are disjoint
  - BBABA's actual parallel mac+linux execution — separate pace
  - Reslating BBABA's docket — its parallel-runs claim becomes accurate once this lands
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Test-only scope is load-bearing; production behavior unchanged
  - Default semantics for unset BURS_TINCTURE (empty vs required) decided at mount time

b2ffca63  ₣BB       Add BURS_TINCTURE per-station tincture (1-3 char lowercase alphanumeric, leading letter, no hyphen, required) to BURS schema; pristine and canonical theurge fixtures compose it into both the throwaway/canonical RBRR cloud and runtime prefixes AND the family stem used for moniker construction, so parallel rbw-tP runs from different stations land in disjoint depot project IDs, GAR repos, GCS buckets, and SA emails on a shared payor manor. Fixture-side mechanism: const RBTDRP_THROWAWAY_CLOUD_PREFIX='prlc-' (and runtime/canonical/family-stem siblings) split into _BASE consts (no tincture, no trailing hyphen) plus composer fns rbtdr{p,k}_{throwaway,canonical}_{cloud,runtime}_prefix(t) / rbtdr{p,k}_family_stem{,_arc}(t); install/probe/moniker-pick sites call the composer with tincture read from BURD_STATION_FILE via rbtdr{p,k}_burs_tincture() (parses station-file BURS_TINCTURE; BURS validation upstream guarantees format). Director/Retriever SA names left unchanged per docket out-of-scope — their emails inherit project-id disjointness once depots disjoint, so 'pristl-ret' and 'canest-ret' SA names are stable across stations. RBRR joint-length validator updated comment-only (no arithmetic change) acknowledging tincture composition behavior; the validator does NOT cross-reference BURS_TINCTURE because BURS isn't kindled in every RBRR-enforce context (rbrr_cli furnish doesn't source burs_regime), so reaching ${BURS_TINCTURE} would trip set -u. Fixture bases are short (≤7 chars: prlc/prlr/canc/canr=4, pristl=6, canest2=7) so tinctured composed values (≤25-26 chars) stay well under the 30-char GCP project-ID cap. Verification: 5 new Rust unit tests covering composer shape, per-tincture disjointness, and dual-station dry-run of full RBDC composition (mirrors rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh:84-87 inline) for project_id, GAR repo, bucket, SA email — all pass; theurge unit suite green at 97/97; fast suite green at 98/98 (regression). 685d58ec  ₢BBAAX    Add missing per-nameplate compose fragment for moriah — second BBAAX surface uncovered by the auto-summon repair (with charge now bootstrapping correctly, the next layer of broken config emerged: 'exec /usr/local/bin/rbid: operation not permitted' on case 3 of moriah's ifrit-attacks section). Diagnosis chain: arch hypothesis ruled out (both ifrit-bottle images are linux/arm64 matching the apple-silicon host); binary inspect identical (794072 bytes, -rwxr-xr-x, file cap cap_net_raw=ep); reproduction in a fresh container with --cap-drop=NET_RAW reproduced the EPERM for both tadmor's AND moriah's binaries; the asymmetry was external — tadmor has .rbk/tadmor/compose.yml with 'cap_add: NET_RAW' that undoes rbob_compose.yml:82-83's 'cap_drop: NET_RAW' on the bottle service. Without that override the file cap can't promote into the effective set (kernel refuses execve with EPERM since file caps cannot exceed the bounding set). Git history confirms .rbk/moriah/compose.yml has never existed (vs .rbk/tadmor/compose.yml landed in A6AAD/AUAAv) — long-standing latent gap that was reachable only after BBAAX's auto-summon repair let charge succeed. Moriah's compose fragment mirrors tadmor's: same user/working_dir/env_file/environment/cap_add/volumes (ifrit bottle, same rbid binary, same Python attack scripts mounted from Tools/rbk/rbtid), with header noting the airgap variant and env_file path swapped to moriah/rbrn.env. srjcl and pluml not addressed — they're non-ifrit bottles (jupyter, plantuml respectively), don't run rbid, and may not need any compose override; if their fixtures fail later they get separate diagnosis. Forbids wrapping tabtarget invocations with tee/tail/head/grep/2>&1/any pipe, since tabtargets self-log to ../logs-buk/ AND zsh defaults pipefail OFF (so a 'tabtarget | tail' pipeline returns tail's exit code of 0 even when the tabtarget failed — a failing test reports as success). Section names the three log files (last.txt, same-{cmd}.txt, hist-{cmd}-{ts}.txt), gives wrong/right examples, and provides escape hatch (set -o pipefail or PIPESTATUS) for rare cases that need live filtering.
